summary: The paper tackles the perception bottleneck in computer-use agents, arguing that prevailing GUI grounding datasets provide only sparse, task-specific supervision that labels a handful of relevant elements per screen and therefore fails to represent the full structural state of an interface. To move toward complete screen parsing, the authors construct ScreenParse, a large-scale dataset of 771K web screenshots with roughly 21M densely annotated UI elements across 55 semantic classes, each carrying a bounding box, a type label, and text. The data is produced by Webshot, an automated and scalable pipeline that renders diverse URLs, extracts DOM-aligned annotations, and applies VLM-based relabeling and quality filtering. Using this data, the authors train ScreenVLM, a compact 316M-parameter vision-language model that decodes a structured ScreenTag markup with a structure-aware loss that upweights layout- and type-critical tokens. Empirically, ScreenVLM outperforms substantially larger prompted foundation VLMs on dense parsing metrics and transfers to public grounding benchmarks, and fine-tuning existing foundation VLMs and detector-based models on ScreenParse consistently improves their performance, which the authors interpret as evidence that dense screen supervision provides transferable structural priors for UI understanding.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The central contribution, the ScreenParse dataset, is a genuine strength. At 771K web screenshots and roughly 21M densely annotated elements across 55 semantic classes, it is considerably larger and more fine-grained than prior complete-annotation resources, and the Webshot pipeline that produces it is well-engineered, combining DOM-aligned extraction with heuristic filtering and VLM-based relabeling into a scalable, low-cost annotation process; releasing the stage-wise prompts in the appendix is commendable for reproducibility. The most convincing empirical result is that the value of the data is not tied to the authors' own model: fine-tuning several existing foundation VLMs (e.g., InternVL3, Qwen3-VL) and detector-based models (e.g., OmniParser) on ScreenParse consistently improves their performance across in-domain and out-of-distribution benchmarks, which substantiates the claim that dense screen supervision supplies transferable structural priors. ScreenVLM is also a nice demonstration of efficiency: a compact 316M-parameter model that outperforms much larger prompted VLMs on dense parsing while offering clear latency and throughput advantages, making it plausible for on-device use. The structure-aware loss is well-motivated, and the ablations showing that upweighting layout and type tokens over text tokens improves out-of-distribution behavior are informative. The problem framing, the shift from sparse, action-oriented grounding to holistic screen parsing, is articulated clearly, and the evaluation metrics (PageIoU, Recall@50, PixCov) are defined precisely enough to follow.

On soundness, the main gap is that the paper repeatedly motivates complete parsing as a prerequisite for computer-use agents to plan and act, yet the evaluation is confined to perception metrics; there is no downstream or end-to-end agentic experiment (e.g., on OSWorld, Mind2Web, or WebArena, or on broader grounding suites such as OSWorld-G, MMBench-GUI, or ScreenSpot-Pro) showing that improved dense parsing translates into higher task success. The gap between a better PageIoU and better agent behavior is non-trivial and currently unvalidated. Annotation quality is likewise under-verified: because the labels are produced automatically from DOM data, which is notoriously noisy due to ads, overlays, and hidden elements, and are filtered by a VLM judge, the paper would benefit from quantitative validation such as a human audit, per-class precision/recall, or annotation-noise statistics, beyond the brief mention of held-out human calibration. Relatedly, the low Label PageIoU values across the board (e.g., 0.197 on ScreenParse, 0.043 on GroundCUA) indicate that element typing, as opposed to spatial coverage, is a real weakness, and a per-class breakdown would help diagnose whether the problem is confusion among similar types or systematic failure on rare classes. The web-only construction also tempers the generalization claims: transfer to native interfaces shows clear domain gaps, with Recall@50 dropping to roughly 0.066 on ScreenSpot Mobile and 0.222 on PC, so while high PixCov suggests the model roughly localizes elements, it does not draw precise boundaries on native UIs. The baseline discussion is somewhat one-sided as well: detector models such as OmniParser and RT-DETRv2 outperform ScreenVLM on exact localization (mAP@50), and dismissing them as not producing language-grounded states is unconvincing given that practical agent stacks routinely pair a detector with an LLM, so a direct justification of the end-to-end VLM over a Detector+LLM pipeline would strengthen the argument. Finally, two data-collection choices deserve scrutiny: capturing only the top-of-page viewport introduces a spatial bias against lower-page and bottom-anchored elements, and hard-filtering containers that exceed 50% of the viewport sits in tension with the stated goal of capturing hierarchical structure, while pure DOM reliance leaves canvas-based applications (e.g., Google Docs, Figma) largely out of scope.

In terms of originality, the dataset and the complete-parsing formulation are the novel core; the modeling component is more incremental, adapting existing document-to-markup VLM architectures with relatively modest changes. The related-work coverage also omits two closely relevant lines, CogAgent's dual-resolution approach to dense UI and tiny-text parsing, and the Ferret-UI family (notably the compact, on-device Ferret-UI Lite), which target very similar problems and should be positioned against. Presentation is generally clear and well-organized, but Figure 2 is hard to read (small in-box text and excessive whitespace), and there is an inconsistency in the reported VLM-as-a-judge acceptance threshold (Figure 2 shows "90" while the appendix states "0.70") that should be reconciled.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. The paper motivates complete parsing as a prerequisite for agent planning and action, but evaluates only perception metrics. Do you have results showing that ScreenVLM, or models fine-tuned on ScreenParse, improve success on end-to-end agentic benchmarks (e.g., OSWorld, Mind2Web, WebArena) or on broader grounding suites (e.g., OSWorld-G, MMBench-GUI, ScreenSpot-Pro)?
2. Can you provide a quantitative assessment of annotation quality, such as a human audit, per-class precision/recall, inter-annotator agreement, or noise statistics, and give more detail on the held-out human validation used to calibrate the VLM-as-a-judge thresholds?
3. Given that detector models outperform ScreenVLM on exact localization (mAP@50), what is the concrete advantage of the end-to-end ScreenVLM over a mature Detector+LLM pipeline for producing language-grounded screen states?
4. How do the top-of-page-only viewport capture and the >50% container hard-filter affect coverage of lower-page elements, hierarchical split-screen layouts and overlays, and canvas-based web applications, and how might these constrain generalization?
5. For reproducibility and robustness, could you clarify (a) how train/validation/test splits avoid URL or domain overlap, (b) the sensitivity of results to the structure-aware loss weights, and (c) whether coordinate quantization (500-bin grid) hurts precise localization of tightly packed elements on high-resolution screens?
limitations: The web-versus-native domain gap is acknowledged, which is the most consequential limitation. The discussion would nonetheless be more complete if it explicitly noted the pipeline-level constraints that shape what the model can learn, namely the reliance on the DOM (which misses canvas-based applications), the top-of-page-only viewport capture, and the filtering of large container elements, and if it added a brief broader-impact note on the dual-use nature of GUI parsing models (e.g., potential misuse for aggressive automated scraping).
